questions: An international collaboration between the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) and UNFPA produced an online compendium of methods for demographic estimation from limited, deficient, and defective data. This resource follows in the intellectual lineage of the United Nations’ Manual X on indirect techniques and later manuals on adult mortality estimation. Within this collection, identify the chapter that focuses specifically on the evaluation and adjustment of fertility data. Provide the chapter’s author, year of publication, full suggested citation (including all listed editors, book title, place of publication, and publisher), and the direct URL where the chapter can be accessed.

A major international collaboration between a global population studies union and the United Nations Population Fund produced a comprehensive volume updating indirect demographic estimation techniques, continuing the methodological lineage of the UN’s Manual X and later manuals on adult mortality. Within this volume, identify (1) the chapter that explains demographic estimation approaches relying on data from only a single population census, and (2) the chapter that focuses specifically on estimating deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth. For each chapter, provide the full chapter title, the author, the year of publication, and the complete suggested citation details (including editors and place of publication).

rounds.answer_a:
The international collaboration between the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) and UNFPA produced the online compendium *Tools for Demographic Estimation* (2013) to build upon the legacy of the United Nations’ *Manual X*. Within this comprehensive manual, the key chapter focusing specifically on the evaluation and adjustment of fertility data is "The Brass P/F ratio method" [1]. 

This chapter, alongside foundational chapters like the "Introduction to fertility analysis" and "Evaluation of data on recent fertility," details how researchers can apply indirect demographic techniques to correct structurally deficient fertility data. Specifically, it demonstrates how to utilize cohort parity data to evaluate and subsequently adjust the level of reported recent fertility [1]. The primary author for these fertility estimation and evaluation chapters is Thomas A. Moultrie. Published in 2013, this resource stands as the modern open-access counterpart to earlier UN indirect estimation manuals [2]. 

The full, official suggested citation for the main methodological chapter is:
Moultrie TA. 2013. "The Brass P/F ratio method." In Moultrie TA, RE Dorrington, AG Hill, K Hill, IM Timæus and B Zaba (eds). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.

Because data evaluation and subsequent adjustment are inextricably linked within demographic estimation, researchers also rely heavily on the preceding evaluation chapter:
Moultrie TA. 2013. "Evaluation of data on recent fertility." In Moultrie TA, RE Dorrington, AG Hill, K Hill, IM Timæus and B Zaba (eds). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population [2].

The explicit step-by-step methodologies provided in this resource have effectively modernized classic estimation techniques for contemporary demographic datasets. 

The direct URL for accessing the core chapter on the evaluation and adjustment of fertility data is: http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/brass-pf-ratio-method

## References

[1] Moultrie, T. A. (2013). The Brass P/F ratio method. In T. A. Moultrie, R. E. Dorrington, A. G. Hill, K. Hill, I. M. Timæus, & B. Zaba (Eds.), *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/brass-pf-ratio-method

[2] Moultrie, T. A., Dorrington, R. E., Hill, A. G., Hill, K., Timæus, I. M., & Zaba, B. (Eds.). (2013). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP). http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/
rounds.answer_b:
The online compendium described is **Tools for Demographic Estimation** (also known as the "IUSSP/UNFPA online manual"), which was developed as a collaborative project between the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) and the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA). This resource serves as a modern successor to the United Nations' *Manual X: Indirect Techniques for Demographic Estimation* (1983) and subsequent manuals addressing adult mortality estimation from deficient data [1][2].

Within this collection, the chapter that focuses specifically on the evaluation and adjustment of fertility data is titled **"Evaluation and adjustment of fertility data"**, authored by **Tom Moultrie**. It was published in **2013** as part of the larger compendium [1][2].

The full suggested citation for this chapter, as provided by the resource itself, is:

**Moultrie TA. 2013. "Evaluation and adjustment of fertility data", in Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM and Zaba B (eds).** ***Tools for Demographic Estimation.*** **Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.** [1][2]

The editors of the full volume are thus Tom A. Moultrie, Rob E. Dorrington, Allan G. Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Basia Zaba. The place of publication is Paris, and the publisher is the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) [1][2].

The direct URL where this chapter can be accessed is:

**http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/evaluation-and-adjustment-fertility-data** [1][2]

This chapter provides detailed guidance on methods for assessing the quality of fertility data collected from censuses and surveys, and techniques for adjusting such data when they are found to be deficient, drawing on both classical and more recent demographic methods.

## References

[1] Tools for Demographic Estimation, IUSSP. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/

[2] Moultrie TA. 2013. "Evaluation and adjustment of fertility data", in Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM and Zaba B (eds). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/evaluation-and-adjustment-fertility-data

Agent B provides the correct chapter title, author, editors, book title, place (Paris), publisher (IUSSP), and direct URL. However, Agent B lists the year as 2013 instead of the correct 2011, which is a factual sub-point error.

Finally, User Experience: Agent B is clearer, better structured, uses bold formatting, and directly answers all required components in a scannable way. Agent A is more verbose, less precise, and centers on the wrong chapter.

Because Agent B has a minor factual error (year), the verdict is capped at BETTER rather than MUCH_BETTER. Agent A failed both Deep Logic (wrong chapter) and Wide completeness (incorrect details).

rounds.answer_b:
The comprehensive volume in question is **Tools for Demographic Estimation**, produced through the collaboration between the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) and the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA), published in 2013. This resource updates and extends the methodological traditions established by the UN's *Manual X: Indirect Techniques for Demographic Estimation* (1983) and subsequent manuals on adult mortality estimation [1][2].

## Chapter 1: Demographic Estimation from a Single Census

The chapter that explains demographic estimation approaches relying on data from only a single population census is titled **"Demographic estimation from a single census"**, authored by **Tom Moultrie**. It was published in **2013** and provides guidance on methods for extracting demographic indicators — including fertility, mortality, and migration estimates — when only one census is available, rather than two successive censuses [1][3].

The full suggested citation is:

**Moultrie TA. 2013. "Demographic estimation from a single census", in Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM and Zaba B (eds).** ***Tools for Demographic Estimation.*** **Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.** [1][3]

## Chapter 2: Estimation of Maternal Mortality

The chapter focusing specifically on estimating deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth is titled **"Estimation of maternal mortality"**, authored by **Basia Zaba**. It was also published in **2013** and covers both direct and indirect techniques for measuring maternal mortality from census and survey data, particularly in settings where vital registration systems are incomplete [1][4].

The full suggested citation is:

**Zaba B. 2013. "Estimation of maternal mortality", in Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM and Zaba B (eds).** ***Tools for Demographic Estimation.*** **Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.** [1][4]

Both chapters share the same editorial team — Tom A. Moultrie, Rob E. Dorrington, Allan G. Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Basia Zaba — and the same place of publication (Paris) and publisher (IUSSP). These chapters can be accessed through the project's dedicated website at demographicestimation.iussp.org [1][2].

## References

[1] Tools for Demographic Estimation, IUSSP. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/

[2] Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM and Zaba B (eds). 2013. *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.

[3] Moultrie TA. 2013. "Demographic estimation from a single census", in Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM and Zaba B (eds). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: IUSSP. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/demographic-estimation-single-census

[4] Zaba B. 2013. "Estimation of maternal mortality", in Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM and Zaba B (eds). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: IUSSP. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/estimation-maternal-mortality
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent B correctly identified the core volume (*Tools for Demographic Estimation*, IUSSP/UNFPA, 2013) and its lineage from UN Manual X. Agent A provided no response, thus completely failing the core entity identification. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent B correctly detailed the census-based chapter (title, author Tom Moultrie, year, full citation with editors and place). However, for the maternal mortality chapter, Agent B incorrectly identified the author (Basia Zaba) and slightly misstated the chapter title; the correct chapter is "Maternal mortality" by Kenneth Hill. This is a significant sub-point error under the WIDE checklist. Agent A, by contrast, failed all checklist items.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Agent B’s answer is well-structured, uses headers, provides full citation details, includes references, and is highly scannable—strong search-agent UX. Agent A offered no usable output.

Because Agent B has a factual error in one sub-point, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER. However, since Agent A completely failed the task (DEEP failure), Agent B is clearly superior overall.
